text: Competitive and elite runners may experience different injuries than recreational runners due to differences in training load, biomechanics, and running experience.

Injury-specific rehabilitation protocols are insufficient for competitive runners because they do not address the broader goal of return to competitive running, including psychosocial and cardiorespiratory fitness needs.
Psychosocial preparedness should be formally evaluated as part of a competitive runner's return-to-competition protocol.
Cardiorespiratory fitness should be monitored throughout rehabilitation in injured runners, with careful interpretation of the data given detraining effects.
A six-phase training paradigm can guide competitive runners from early injury stages through full return to competition, providing structured progression milestones.

Return-to-running rehabilitation for competitive athletes should be comprehensive, integrating musculoskeletal recovery, cardiorespiratory reconditioning, and psychosocial readiness rather than treating each in isolation.
